skill.name: gget
skill.description: Unified CLI/Python interface for querying genomic, proteomic, structure, and expression data across 20+ bioinformatics databases; use when you need fast, scriptable retrieval by gene/protein IDs or keywords.
skill.summary: Query genomic, proteomic, and expression data from 20+ bioinformatics databases using a unified command-line interface.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 48 static analysis findings were evaluated. High-severity findings (weak crypto, certificate/key files) are false positives caused by the static analyzer misidentifying Markdown code spans and Python variable names. Medium-severity shell backtick execution findings are false positives from Markdown inline code formatting and code-block documentation examples. Low-severity hardcoded URL findings are confirmed but benign (links to official gget documentation and GitHub repository). The CRITICAL heuristic combination finding (code execution + network) was dismissed because network calls to public bioinformatics databases are the documented purpose of this skill, and no credential access or obfuscation was found. The only confirmed risk factors are external_commands (running python scripts/wrapper.py) and network (gget library API calls to Ensembl, UniProt, NCBI, and other public bioinformatics databases), both of which are expected and transparently documented behaviors.

high_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm (False Positive)
high_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 4 instances of weak cryptographic algorithms. Evaluation confirmed these are false positives: no cryptographic operations exist in any file. The scanner likely confused Markdown formatting patterns or argument names (e.g., 'args' suggesting 'AES'/'DES') with cryptographic algorithm references.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Files contain no imports, function calls, or string references to any cryptographic library. The wrapper.py file only imports argparse, json, sys, and pandas.

high_findings.title: Certificate/Key File Reference (False Positive)
high_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged args.keywords at scripts/wrapper.py:27 as a certificate/key file. This is a Python argument parser attribute access for command-line keyword arguments, not a certificate or key reference.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged token 'keywords' is clearly a variable attribute on an argparse Namespace object, with no file path, certificate data, or key material nearby.

medium_findings.title: Ruby/Shell Backtick Execution (False Positive)
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged inline Markdown code spans (e.g., 'keywords', 'species', 'ens_ids') and code-block examples (bash code fences) as shell backtick execution. These are documentation formatting conventions, not executed shell commands. No actual command injection or shell execution via backticks occurs in the skill.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged locations are inside Markdown code fences or inline code spans. The skill executes Python scripts through subprocess, not shell backticks. Line 16 of module_reference.md contains only '‘keywords’, ‘species’' as parameter documentation.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs in Documentation
low_findings.description: Three hardcoded URLs found in documentation files. These link to the official gget documentation site, the gget GitHub repository, and the source skill repository. These are benign reference URLs providing attribution and documentation sources.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: URLs are clearly visible in the source and point to legitimate academic/open-source resources. The gget library is a well-known bioinformatics tool from the Pachter Lab at Caltech.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Patterns (False Positive)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged field names in gget_audit_result_v1.json (lines 35, 115, 120, 155, 190) as system reconnaissance. These are data field names in an audit results JSON file describing bioinformatics operations (search, info, seq), not reconnaissance commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The JSON file is a pre-computed audit result. Field names like 'search', 'info', 'seq' describe the bioinformatics module operations evaluated in the audit, not active system reconnaissance.

content.value_statement: Researchers need to retrieve genomic, proteomic, and expression data from multiple public databases, but each database has a different interface and query format. This skill provides a unified command-line interface to query 20+ bioinformatics databases using gene or protein identifiers and keywords, enabling scriptable and reproducible data retrieval workflows.
actual_capabilities: Search genes and proteins by keyword across Ensembl, UniProt, and NCBI databases
Retrieve detailed gene and protein metadata (annotations, descriptions, identifiers)
Fetch nucleotide and amino acid sequences for one or more gene identifiers
Download or predict protein structures using PDB retrieval and AlphaFold
Query gene expression data from ARCHS4, CELLxGENE, and Bgee databases
Perform enrichment analysis on gene lists using the Enrichr platform
limitations: Requires gget Python library to be installed in the execution environment
AlphaFold structure prediction may have long execution times and environment-specific dependencies
Network access to external databases is required and queries may time out with large datasets
Does not support real-time streaming or interactive visualization of results

use_cases.title: Retrieve gene information for a research project
use_cases.description: A molecular biologist needs to look up detailed annotations for a list of Ensembl gene IDs to understand their function and associated pathways.

use_cases.title: Fetch protein sequences for structural analysis
use_cases.description: A bioinformatics researcher needs nucleotide or protein sequences for a set of genes to run downstream structural prediction or phylogenetic analysis.

use_cases.title: Query gene expression across tissue types
use_cases.description: A computational biologist wants to check expression levels of a gene across different tissues from public RNA-seq datasets to validate experimental results.

prompt_templates.title: Search for genes by keyword
prompt_templates.prompt: Search for genes related to insulin in human using the gget tool.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner user searching for genes by keyword across databases

prompt_templates.title: Retrieve gene metadata by Ensembl ID
prompt_templates.prompt: Fetch detailed information for Ensembl gene ID ENSG00000141510 using gget.

prompt_templates.title: Fetch protein sequences for multiple genes
prompt_templates.prompt: Retrieve protein sequences for Ensembl IDs ENSG00000141510, ENSG00000130203 using gget.
prompt_templates.scenario: Researcher gathering sequence data for downstream analysis

prompt_templates.title: Query expression data and run enrichment analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Query expression data for gene TP53 from the ARCHS4 database and perform enrichment analysis on the top co-expressed genes using gget.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user combining expression queries with enrichment analysis

output_examples.input: Search for gene TP53 in human using gget.
output: Description: Tumor suppressor protein involved in cell cycle regulation and apoptosis

output_examples.input: Fetch protein sequence for Ensembl ID ENSG00000141510 using gget.

best_practices: Use specific Ensembl or UniProt identifiers instead of keywords when possible for more precise and faster results.
Start with a search command to find the correct identifiers before running info or seq commands.
Install the gget library in a dedicated Python environment to avoid dependency conflicts with other tools.
anti_patterns: Do not pass ambiguous or very short keywords to search commands as this returns too many results to process effectively.
Avoid running multiple large sequence retrieval queries in parallel to prevent timeout errors and rate limiting.
Do not use this skill for real-time clinical decision-making without validating results against primary sources.

faq.question: What databases does gget support?
faq.answer: gget supports Ensembl, UniProt, NCBI, ARCHS4, CELLxGENE, Bgee, Enrichr, PDB, and AlphaFold EBI API among others.

faq.question: Do I need to install any dependencies?
faq.answer: Yes, you need to install the gget Python library and pandas. Run 'uv pip install gget pandas' to install them.

faq.question: Can I use gene symbols instead of Ensembl IDs?
faq.answer: Yes, the search command accepts gene symbols and keywords. The info and seq commands accept Ensembl IDs, UniProt IDs, or gene names.

faq.question: Does gget support batch queries?
faq.answer: Yes, you can pass multiple IDs to the info and seq commands using space-separated values with the --ids parameter.

faq.question: How do I handle timeout errors?
faq.answer: Timeout errors can occur with large queries or slow network connections. Try reducing batch sizes or running queries one at a time.

faq.question: Is this skill suitable for clinical use?
faq.answer: No, this skill is designed for research purposes only. Always validate results against primary sources before any clinical application.
